text:
Today you disappeared.

You are alive, I know it, because I can feel it with all my strength.

Mom does not know yet that they took you.



It was before everything changed. Dad had come home from work exhausted, as he so often did. Mom seemed just as tired as he was, and the atmosphere at the table was not exactly light. You and I would look at each other, watching for a word from one, a sentence from the other that would unleash the storm. It was almost a game between us, to be the one who would be first to wink when we were sure the moment had come. In your room there was a jar of candy; whoever won had the right to help themselves. It was our way of making peace with joy, of forgetting by bedtime what we believed back then were only the wars that grown-ups fight. Today I wish I could still hear them shouting, Mom's sighs, see our father flee the fight by taking the dog for a walk. Today I wish everything could go back to the way it was before. Before one man's madness spread its bloody mists across our land.



When you did not appear at the time you come home every evening, I understood that something had happened to you, I knew that the monsters had taken you in their clutches. I ran until I was out of breath, promising God that if I was wrong and saw you in the schoolyard, sitting alone on the bench as sometimes happens when the day has been too hard, or in the infirmary because you had skinned your knee again in a fight you had lost, I would believe in him forever. I passed Mrs. Blansky's house, its shutters closed, then I quickened my pace. Running past the ruins of the building where Mr. Zillig, the pianist, had lived, I could not remember whether it had had seven or eight stories, and that lapse filled me with a terrible rage. How can you forget such a thing so quickly... as if the happy days had been swept away forever.

1



His head tilted to one side, Valentyn studies the man. When he watches someone, that position gives him an interesting perspective, an angle that lets him see more. Perhaps that is only a pretext to justify a precocious quirk. At nine, anything out of the ordinary gets called that. At chess as at the piano, Valentyn is precocious; he is in mathematics too, but what is most precocious of all is his intuition, an exceptional ability to guess what people are thinking. The only area where he is seriously behind is speech. Selective mutism in children, a temporary block, Dr. Zablonsky had assured Valentyn's parents, though as his sixth birthday approached, he still had not uttered a word. Zablonsky, an excellent pediatrician, was not the sort to settle for a hypothetical diagnosis, especially when the problem was delicate. He explored every possible lead, looked for the slightest correlation between the symptoms, and his pride did not keep him from asking a colleague's opinion. After having his young patient examined by a speech specialist and sending his file to a neurologist, he had been categorical. The child had impeccable hearing, his intellectual development was above average, and the report from an MRI scan had confirmed that his brain was absolutely normal. If Valentyn had been able to speak, he would have asked Zablonsky what a normal brain was. At least staying quiet spared him from asking stupid questions.

That morning, the man pretending to choose a box of cereal in the aisles of the little grocery store is behaving strangely. Valentyn would swear he is following him. Two weeks earlier, he had noticed him on the sidewalk opposite the dispensary. The guy had taken forever to open a pack of chewing gum, which still was not exactly a complicated operation; stranger still, when Valentyn came level with him, he had slipped the packet into his pocket without taking a single piece.

A few days later, Valentyn thought he recognized him again, this time loitering at the bus stop. But buses stopped running long ago, everyone knows that, so why hang around like that? In any case, he was not from around here. Valentyn knows more or less everyone; very few faces in the neighborhood are unfamiliar to him. He should have shared his worry with his sister, he thinks, but Lilya already has enough troubles as it is, and besides, maybe he is imagining things. Valentyn has a world of his own, one he fills with imaginary beings whenever he is bored, a world where extraordinary adventures follow one after another if he is still bored, as in class, for example. It is while thinking that, and to reassure himself, that he remembers an important detail. Three days earlier, he saw two other men stationed on the sidewalk; not directly opposite the school, but twenty meters to either side of the entrance, so perfectly symmetrical that he had found it strange.

The memory makes his heart beat a little faster. He slips off his backpack to take out his communication notebook. A spiral notebook he uses to communicate with the people around him. He grabs the first ballpoint pen he finds in his pencil case and behaves as though he were writing a shopping list. He writes a note to his math teacher, his first class of the day, puts his things away, and heads, as if nothing were wrong, toward the shelves of canned goods at the very back of the store. With a quick glance, he checks that the guy has not followed him, gently pushes open the door at the rear of the shop, and goes out. The advantage of the terrain, his father, whom he has not seen in long months, would have said.

Once outside, he tears off at full speed, veers into an alley, slips under the fence around the vacant lot, and reaches the school by the shortcut he takes on mornings when he has lingered in bed a little too long.

Valentyn does not linger in the playground. He passes his classmates without greeting them, darts into the old brick building, and runs up the stairs. On the first floor, he stops short outside the door of his classroom to think for a moment.

If he shows what he has written, he risks looking like someone trying to show off. Suspecting a man because he had trouble opening a pack of chewing gum and turned up again at the grocery store, or two others because they lingered on a sidewalk, is not enough to alarm the people around him;

but Valentyn is certain his instinct is not misleading him. If nature has condemned him to muteness, it has given him, in return, a power of perception beyond the ordinary. Summoning all his courage, he carefully copies the latest conclusions of his investigation into his notebook, goes into the classroom, and lays his prose on the teacher's desk.

After reading it, Mrs. Jaruski raises her head and studies him closely. Valentyn shrugs, resolved to be scolded. But he is not the reason for the severe look on his teacher's face.

"You did the right thing bringing me this. I'll speak to my colleagues about it. This evening, don't linger on your way home. If you want, I can call your mother and have her come pick you up."

Valentyn shakes his head no.

"As you wish, but stay on your guard. And if you spot any of these men again, come and tell me at once. Now you can go sit down," she adds, handing back his notebook, "the bell will ring soon."

Valentyn’s instincts have not misled him; this will not be a school day like any other.

At a middle school four streets away, Lilya, sitting at her desk, rolls the pencil she has been chewing between her fingers, her eyes fixed on the peeling paint on the classroom walls. The windows, whitened by dust clumped there by the rain, are still intact. A miracle, because there are not many buildings left in the neighborhood with unbroken windows. The principal has assured them that schools are safe places, that the barbarians who bombard civilian homes would not make war on children. The principal is a gentle dreamer, Lilya has no doubt of that. To the west, the barbarians shelled a maternity hospital and, in Marinka, a shell tore through the wall of a ground-floor nursery school before exploding in a playroom. Fortunately, the attack came at lunchtime and the cafeteria is on the second floor. No dead, but many injured. How do you explain to a four-year-old that men hidden dozens of kilometers away deliberately targeted them? The principal lied: schools are not sanctuaries.

Lilya’s gaze wanders from desk to desk, hopping like a sparrow that cannot fly away. At last the bird comes to rest on the nape of Stefan’s neck, where he sits in the third row.

There is something moving about that boy. The others swagger through the hallways in groups; he trails his long silhouette, along with the extra year from having repeated a grade, with a nonchalance she finds elegant. His presence causes a new sensation in Lilya, a knot that forms in her throat whenever she comes near him; but what troubles her more than anything is the attention with which he listens to her. As if every word she says mattered. At the end of the day, sometimes he walks beside her in silence on the way home, and Lilya, proud as she is of her own boldness, admits to herself that his presence reassures her.

Their first real meeting had taken place one evening when Stefan stepped toward her outside the school gates.

“Are you going home?” he had asked.

“No, I’m picking up my little brother first.”

“Shall I come with you?”

Lilya was dying to.

“There’s no need,” she had answered.

“As you like.”

“Wait, that’s not what you think. My brother’s different.”

“Aren’t we all?”

And before Stefan could ask another question, Lilya had told him the truth.

“He doesn’t talk.”

“I don’t talk much either.”

“Yes, but he never does.”

Stefan had shrugged, and his answer had surprised Lilya.

“It’s his right, after all. And do we have the right to talk to him?”

“Of course, he answers with gestures or by writing in his notebook.”

“Then your brother is a poet.”

“Yes, something like that.”

Two days later, Stefan had waited for Lilya in the same place. As they walked past the ruins of what had once been a shopping mall, he pulled a small book from his pocket.

“For your brother,” he had said.

Lilya had looked at the cover, a collection of poems by Serhiy Zhadan.

“You know,” Stefan had added, “the most precious thing isn’t the voice, but freedom; I think more people realize that now. The ones invading us have never known it, and that’s why they hate us so much, at least those who hate us.”

He had stopped walking, smiled at Lilya, and turned back. And she, holding a book of poems whose warmth radiated through her hands, had watched him go, her heart full of an ardor she knew nothing about.

Over one or another of those evenings when they came to know each other, without ever revealing it to anyone, a friendship tinged with love had sprung up in their adolescence.

At lunchtime, while her pupils are in the cafeteria, Mrs. Jaruski watches through the window the two buses parked in front of the school entrance. She has been told nothing about any school outing, which in times like these would have been unthinkable anyway. The presence of a canvas-covered truck parked not far from the buses worries her all the more. Suddenly the buses pull away, the rumble of their engines shaking the pane where she has rested her forehead. A foolish fear, she tells herself, but how can she not be afraid when explosions tear through the night, when the sirens wail out of nowhere and she has to lead these children in her care to the shelters, holding herself back from shouting so she will not frighten them, when only the calm of her voice reassures the children. A few months ago Mrs. Jaruski was cursing the reform of school menus that had caused no end of trouble in the cafeteria; today she curses the servants of hatred and oppression.

The canvas-covered truck passes the school again, ahead of the buses. But why drive around the block, Mrs. Jaruski wonders, except to avoid drawing attention too soon? Something is afoot. She leaves her classroom to alert the principal and quickens her pace in the corridor. She still has to climb to the second floor and her lungs are already on fire. She hesitates when she reaches the stairwell; time is short, and to save the children from a danger that feels imminent she will have to show initiative.

Instead of going up, she rushes down the stairs to the ground floor. A fit of coughing stops her on the landing. She is struggling to breathe; her doctor has begged her to take it easy, but this is no time to obey him. Twenty more metres, she presses her elbows against her chest, like a marathon runner at the end of a race; her legs wobble. In the distance she hears men shouting, doors slamming. She flings open the cafeteria door. Out of breath, incapable of uttering a word, she casts a desperate look at the supervisor making sure the meal proceeds calmly. The cook, busy cleaning her dishes, sees Mrs. Jaruski's stricken face and understands the urgency of the situation. She orders the children to get up while the math teacher slowly comes back to her senses.

"Leave your things, run to the gym, and get out of the school through the emergency exit; once you're outside, go straight home without stopping and don't come back until you've been told to, understood? Come on, off you go, clear out!" the cook shouts.

But the children do not understand a thing. The sirens that announce an air raid have not sounded, and why the gym instead of going down to the cellar, as they do every time? Mrs. Jaruski claps her hands, pushes toward the exit those who have stood up, too few of them. The cook smashes the glass on the fire alarm and yanks the handle.

As soon as the bells start ringing, the cafeteria finally empties. The children rush into the corridor toward the swinging doors of the gym.

Cosima is lagging behind, not out of unwillingness, but because she too is different. Her artificial leg makes her limp. The orthopedist has promised her that once she has reached her full height, a more modern prosthesis will let her walk like everyone else, even run; but Cosima will have to be patient, until she grows and her country is freed from the oppressors.

Valentyn refuses to leave her behind. Cosima, for her part, refuses to let anyone take her by the arm and, more generally, to accept help getting around. So he settles for walking beside her, matching his pace to hers. When he hears voices behind him, he turns and finds a strange sight. The cook and Mrs. Jaruski are trying to block the men's way with their bodies. Mrs. Jaruski is as thin as a matchstick, but the cook has impressed plenty of others before now; even the principal is no match for her. She has authority even in her eyes, and when she plants her hands on her hips, whoever stands up to her is about to be in serious trouble. So when Valentyn sees her go crashing backward, pushed back manu militari by a uniformed man, he cannot believe it, and if he had been able to speak, he would have told Cosima they had to hurry. He breaks the rule and grabs his best friend by the hand all the way to the gym.

The cook has lost the fight, but the trick she and Mrs. Jaruski used has worked all the same, because all the other children managed to get away. In the deserted gym, Valentyn points out the emergency exit to Cosima, behind the pole of the basketball hoop.

Cosima, paralyzed with fear, is trembling all over. Valentyn understands that they will never reach it in time. At once he thinks of his father, from whom he has had no news since he left for the front. What would he have done in the same circumstances? The answer seems obvious. He points to the basketball hoop again, smiles at Cosima as he pushes her toward the exit, and turns back. He is going to hold the enemy off as long as possible.

And yet when the soldiers seize him, he has fought like a devil, running them ragged as he zigzags through the bleachers; Valentyn turns one last time to watch the strip of light growing narrower on the floor as the emergency exit swings shut.

The two buses that were supposed to take around a hundred children to an unknown destination will carry only two. Valentyn and one of his classmates, who had the bad luck to linger in the bathroom.

What drives men to feed on lies? Veronika wonders. Perhaps because, more than God, they fear facing their own truth. During her break, the head nurse of the Rykove dispensary has no choice but to accept hers. If the men occupying her town were to win their war, her country would disappear, and its memory with it. The invaders need to erase the past, rewrite History to justify their ideology and wipe away their crimes. In the writings of the historians of Putin's regime, the crimes of the Soviet system, of which millions of Russians were victims, have been forgotten, the mass deportations turned into mere internments or relocations. It is in the general interest, the advocates of forgetting argue, for victims to live peacefully side by side with their executioners. They are so afraid of the duty to remember that would keep such atrocities from happening again. But History is made of nothing but the little stories of the people who lived. How many testimonies have already vanished with those whom Veronika has covered with a sheet in the dispensary's emergency ward? Two hundred bodies buried on the outskirts of town since the beginning of the invasion. As many lives lost, parents and grandparents who will pass nothing more on to their children and grandchildren. On the graveyards of vanished memories, nothing will grow now but thistles of hatred.

Her pager vibrates at her waist; she barely has time to glance at it.

"We've got a new arrival, in bad shape," her colleague says, coming into the break room. "You know smoking is forbidden, even by the window."

Veronika crushes out her cigarette, dreaming, at the same hour every day, of the opening of a new Nuremberg1 in Simferopol, in a liberated Crimea. In the meantime, her break is over and, with the operation ahead, her shift is far from done, unless the patient dies. She looks at her watch; in two hours Lilya will pick up Valentyn from school. Her children are a constant trial to her, her son walled up in silence and her daughter thrust too soon to the threshold of adolescence. She feels guilty for having almost wished the operation would end prematurely and resigns herself to seeing them only after dinner, as has often been the case since the start of the war. Late that night, she will kiss them in their beds and pray with all her strength that no explosion comes to disturb their sleep. Now that the city has been captured, the nights are calmer, but here everyone is watching for the counteroffensive.

She stops at the scrub sinks, careful not to use more disinfectant than necessary, pulls on her gown and ties her mask before entering the operating room. Two wounded men are lying on the table, one around fifty, the other barely twenty.

"They were coming back from the fields. A mercenary shot at their car," the surgeon says.

"Why?" Veronika asks curtly.

"For no reason, because Wagner's men like killing. They like it so much they've made a profession of it. Prigozhin2 sold the services of his private militias to Bashar al-Assad to help him massacre the Syrian population; in Africa, he has made a fortune by attaching himself to bloody coups. When his men are short of work, he sends them to seize the continent's riches. Blood-diamond mines or cobalt mines. Putin is by far his biggest client; with all the Ukrainians they murder, I imagine Wagner's army must be getting bonuses. I wouldn't be surprised if one day Prigozhin ended up killing the Tsar too, to sit on his throne. In the meantime, I can't take care of both victims at once; the father looks to be in worse shape than the son, so I'll start with him."

"But the other one is much younger," Veronika objects, "and he has a bullet in his lung."

"We have to make choices right now. I might be able to save them both, provided you help me instead of arguing."

The anesthetist has done his work; both wounded men are asleep. The surgeon suggests that Veronika watch over the younger man while he operates. He will call on her if need be.

The young man's condition seems stable, although a patient with a penetrating chest wound can deteriorate quickly. If air started building up in his chest, his lung would be compressed and would soon collapse. Veronika would rather not think about what would follow. With no ultrasound machine available, the only precaution is to monitor and listen to his breathing, listen for the first wheeze, or any shortness of breath, and make sure his lips and fingertips do not turn blue.

She prepares the decompression equipment, just in case. A long needle that she will have to insert between the ribs with painstaking dexterity; the slightest deviation would be devastating. This young man's only chance of continuing to live will depend on her.

Thirty minutes have gone by when the surgeon lets out a long sigh. Sweat beads on his brow; he wipes it away and sighs again. Two bullets have pierced the body of the man he is operating on, one in the left leg and one in the abdomen. A battle-seasoned doctor, he saw his share of war wounded during the invasion of Crimea, and he delivers his version of events with the coldness of a medical examiner.

"The bastard shot them like rabbits through the car door. The father was driving, he yelled at his son to get down. The bullet that went through his abdomen came out again and lodged in the body of his passenger," he says, tracing an imaginary path with his finger. "How is he?"

"He's holding on," Veronika answers. "And it isn't necessarily his son," she adds. "Maybe a nephew or a farmhand, or simply some guy from the countryside he picked up in his car. I don't think they look alike."

The head nurse has contradicted her boss to blot out men's savagery; not to think that three seconds were enough, the span of a burst of gunfire and an instant of hatred, to destroy a family, while in this room they will spend hours trying to repair the damage and save two lives. And if they fail, it will fall to her to tell a wife and mother that a son and a husband will never be coming home again.

The principal of Lilya's middle school walks into the classroom, looking stricken. The students stare at him in utter silence. He climbs onto the platform, takes his place beside the teacher, and announces that classes are over for today. An incident has occurred at a nearby primary school. He orders them to leave at once, not to make any detours on the way, and to lock their doors as soon as they get home.

Lilya springs to her feet and asks what kind of incident it was and at which school it happened.

"The closest one to here," the principal replies; he has not found the words to tell her it is the one her brother attends. "The Russians carried out a roundup," he goes on. "Luckily, apart from two children, all the others managed to escape."

"Who are the two pupils who couldn't escape?" Lilya insists, her voice shaking.

"They'll surely release them before this evening..."

Before the principal has finished his sentence, Lilya bolts into the corridor. Never in her life has she run so fast, never has she been so afraid, not even when the mercenaries entered her town firing in every direction. In front of the little house where she lives, she fumbles in her backpack for her keys, pounds on the door, screaming her little brother's name. No answer. She turns her bag upside down and empties it onto the front step. Then she snatches up the key ring she has spotted under a notebook, undoes the latch, and bursts in. She calls out in the entryway, in the living room, as she races upstairs. Valentyn obviously cannot answer her, and if he still hasn't appeared it's because that knucklehead is on his bed, headphones on, playing on his console. She's going to give him a proper scolding, no, she'll throw her arms around him, kiss him like never before, and they'll laugh together at how lucky they were.

When she finds her brother's room deserted, Lilya understands that he isn't at a friend's either, because she has a gift for sensing disaster before it strikes. Lilya was the first to realize that her brother did not speak, and she knew long before her father confided in her that he was going away to the front.

She falls to her knees and lets out a terrible roar. You would think you were hearing a dying beast. She pounds the floor with her fists, screaming, "Not him, I beg you, not him!"

Crying will do no good, she gets back up, races down the stairs, and hurtles toward the school. If she caught sight of him in the yard, sitting alone on a bench the way he sometimes does when the day has been too hard, she would believe in God forever.

She passes the Blanskys' house. Since her husband's death, his widow has always kept the shutters closed. She quickens her pace and runs along the ruins of the apartment building where Valentyn's music teacher lived. Rage in her belly, she lengthens her stride all the way to the primary school yard.

On the bench, Mrs. Jaruski is comforting a cook who cannot be comforted.

One exchange of glances is enough for Lilya to understand.



2



"The father is out of danger, for now," the surgeon says, washing his bloodied hands.

He ought to change his gloves, but everything is done on the cheap.

"Or the uncle or the Good Samaritan," the nurse corrects him.

"You really piss me off, Veronika. And the worst part is, I've come to believe you enjoy it."

"We take our pleasures where we can, Doctor."

"So how is this young man?"

"In the best of shape, as you can see for yourself," she replies.



The surgeon shoots her a blazing look, but stops himself from taking the bait. That is all she is waiting for, for him to lose patience and tear into her. Saving a life has put him in a better mood than usual, even if stitching up civilians wounded by gunfire is anything but usual. He hooks the stethoscope earpieces over his ears, listens for a long time to the lungs and the heartbeat, checks the blood pressure, and makes a dubious moue that, to Veronika, looks more like a strange grimace. Then he kneels to bring himself level with the wound, studies it carefully, and slips a finger inside.

"In another life, I was probably a ballistics expert," he says, proud of himself.

"I don't think that discipline existed in your previous life, since that would still take us back to the end of the nineteenth century," Veronika replies.

"Be as smart as you like, the fact remains that after passing through the vehicle door and the old man's fat, the projectile pierced this young man at greatly reduced velocity. He's a miracle case. It didn't hit the lung. It's lodged between two ribs, I can feel it on the tip of my index finger. If you would be kind enough to hand me a pair of forceps instead of looking at me askance, we could even remove it and stitch this young man back up."



Veronika hands him the forceps she has been holding ever since he bent over the wounded man. Naturally it will fall to him to announce the good news to the family, and as if that were not enough, the old hand was right twice over: he has managed to save both patients, who really are father and son.

"Assuming fate plays the nasty trick of making us work together again in your next life, I've asked to be a ballet dancer; I thought I should warn you... I have a very hard time picturing you in a tutu."

As things turned out, Danylo has become the dispensary's handyman. Before February 24th, his duties were limited to maintenance, which was already a titanic job. He never studied, but he is a formidable fixer. He maintains, repairs, patches things up with whatever comes to hand. The boiler gives him grief every day of winter, and God knows how long winter is in this region. The moment it gets pushed, when the temperatures drop, it gums up, coughs, and the burner goes out. A real old clunker, he grumbles every time he has to somehow find a part that will buy it a little more time. He does surgery too, in his own way, and when he is done operating, his hands are every bit as appetizing as the doc's. Now he is also in charge of cleaning. Between the dead and those who have left, the staff is sorely lacking. His shift should have ended an hour ago; since then, he has been cooling his heels outside the operating room, regularly peering through the porthole window. This time, he tries his luck and eases the door open.

"And are you finished?" he asks.

His habit of starting his sentences with an "And" amuses more than a few people. Several people at the dispensary mimic his verbal tic to mock him; some even refer to him as "And."

Veronika is above such petty little games. She asks him to fetch a stretcher and come back to help her take the patients to recovery.

"And your daughter called, it sounded urgent," says Danylo.

"When did she call?" Veronika asks, alarmed.

"Well, a little while ago," grumbles the handyman, not at all pleased to have to play stretcher-bearer on top of everything else.

He didn't look at his watch, and adds that it wouldn't have changed anything anyway, since the operating room is off-limits during surgery.

The surgeon suggests Veronika leave; he'll take care of the patients and let the family know they are out of danger. As she takes off her gown, Veronika wonders why Lilya called. Lilya never disturbs her when she's at the dispensary. Unless she'd once again got a note from one of her teachers for her to sign for the next day, or another quarrel had broken out with her brother? When Valentyn's thoughts crowd too fast for his pencil to set them down, he sometimes loses his temper. A child who has no words for his anger shows it another way, sometimes by slamming doors, sometimes by breaking things.

A fine rain mingles with the wind whipping her face. In the parking lot darkened by night, Veronika pulls the collar of her coat tighter around her neck. Her house is a good ten-minute walk away, and fuel is too scarce for her to drive her old car to work.

She walks back up the street, crushed with fatigue. She is glad at the thought of seeing her children again, even if Lilya's call makes her dread an evening that won't exactly be restful. She walks slowly to enjoy this moment all to herself, brief as it is, her buffer zone, as she calls it, because naming things makes them exist.

As she crosses the roundabout, she thinks this wretched day has ended rather well. Life has become even harder since the father of her children left. If their life as a couple had already been nothing more than cohabitation, his absence has left a bigger void than she had imagined.

When she goes inside, she finds her daughter sitting on the floor in the middle of the living room, hiccuping, eyes wild.

"What happened now?" asks Veronika.

The news of Valentyn's abduction causes dreadful pain, as if the bullets that mowed down the peasants had suddenly torn through her chest. Her heart pounds; she feels as though she is suffocating.

Lilya bursts into tears. Veronika looks at her; she will not let fear get the upper hand, she has to act, because even in the worst moments she remains a mother who must protect her daughter. So she goes over to Lilya, kneels down, and holds her in her arms, giving her what love she has left, all that tenderness she has been holding back without knowing why, perhaps because life, through sheer exhaustion, has finally walled her in.

It has been so long since Lilya last took refuge in her arms that Veronika feels as though she is going back in time. Against her chest run the tears of the child she used to comfort through nights of nightmares, the child who shared everything with her, her laughter as much as her sorrows. They have taken her son from her; she finds her daughter again.

"We'll go get him tomorrow," she promises. "Tonight you'll sleep in my bed, or I'll sleep in yours, whichever you want."

On the coach, Valentyn had felt full of strength, proud of having saved Cosima, or at least spared her that forced outing. He had no idea where they were going. Maybe they were being taken to visit a Russian village on the other side of the border so that afterward he and his friend could come back and say it was better than home. He had heard it said that truth comes out of the mouths of children, though that was not always the case. Cosima, since he was still thinking about her, was the queen of lies. She invented a new one every time she got to school late. Like when she had claimed her grandmother was very sick, even though the granny had been dead for ages.

When they had taken the road that ran toward the border, he had wondered what dirty trick the Russians had dreamed up. With the racket the old bus was making, their plan was not going to work the way they expected. He knew a thing or two about mechanics. Back when his family still lived in Irpin, on the outskirts of Kyiv, his father had fallen for an old Beetle, and on weekends, when they worked on it together, Valentyn had learned almost everything at his side. The rest he had picked up from books and maintenance manuals. He knew the name of most engine parts and could tell by ear when something was wrong. One summer, when the family had treated themselves to a few days in the country, the Beetle had been making the same kind of groan before it broke down. Valentyn was sure the driver would soon be pulling onto the shoulder. Meanwhile his friend was scared stiff, unable to stop sobbing. He could not get over the humiliation of having been caught with his pants down. He had jumped when the door flew open. He had barely had time to clean himself up before a man grabbed him by the shoulder and dragged him away by force.

Comforting his friend made Valentyn feel stronger still. He had opened his notebook and written that everything would be fine, that there was no reason to worry, and that they would surely be home before nightfall, since the bus was about to give up the ghost. To show off, without being sure how to spell the word, he had written that a connecting rod was about to snap. Unable to hide his satisfaction, when the driver let loose a stream of curses after a thick cloud of smoke billowed out from under the hood, he had bent over a blank page and written in big letters, They're screwed, we'll be home for dinner. But on that point, Valentyn was wrong.

At first light, Veronika and Lilya leave their house and head for the town hall, where the Russian command has set itself up. Veronika has known legendary fits of anger, but never one like the fury driving her now. Invading a country, bombing cities, decimating entire populations to serve the megalomania of a fading dictator was not enough for them? Now they had to kidnap kids as well? Was this what those conquerors had been reduced to under incapable generals, mercenaries making up the battalions of an army without honor?

As for bravery, the soldier standing guard outside the town hall has little left. He does not know what he is doing in this remote corner so far from home. A young man barely twenty-five, happy to be among those who took a small town without having had to wage any major battles, happier still to be alive. He points them toward the office of the superior in charge of the occupied population. The town's next mayor, once Ukraine has been liberated, he explains.

"Excellent news, then you'll be going home soon," Veronika shoots back dryly, pulling her daughter along.

They stride down the corridor with an almost military step, their heels striking so loudly that no one they pass dares ask where they are going. Lilya spots the commandant's office, Veronika takes a deep breath, and opens the door.

"Where is my son?" she asks as she enters the room.

The officer, who had been dozing with his head slumped over a desk, starts and stares at the two women.

"Who is your son?" he asks with a yawn.

"Valentyn Khodova!"

The man straightens up, turns with a sigh, and takes from a shelf the binder where the names of fighters captured or killed at the front are recorded.

"Don't pretend to look for his name. He's nine years old, one of the two children you abducted from their school yesterday at noon. Are you going to pretend you don't know about it?"

His expression changes. He is the one who, a week earlier, received the order to carry out this operation, whose usefulness had at first seemed questionable to him. As if he did not already have enough problems to deal with. But the call had come directly from Moscow, more important still, from the Kremlin. Few officers of his rank were granted such an honor. The call had lasted only a few minutes, but he would never forget the soft, benevolent voice of the president of the Commission for Children's Rights, paying no heed to the power conferred by her closeness to the president. Instead of using the threatening tone to which his superiors had accustomed him, she had carefully explained the importance of what he would have to carry out. What cause could be nobler than protecting orphans and minors from the dangers the Ukrainian rebellion exposes them to? People who accuse Russia of the worst evils, though they are incapable of caring what becomes of their own offspring. Saving orphans was a priority, she had repeated before hanging up.

Orphan, a detail he may have neglected to specify to the unit he dispatched. But then again, why would he have, since he had been assured the place in question was an orphanage?

And yet this mother is demanding her child; perhaps his intelligence sources are not so reliable after all. He will find whoever is responsible for this blunder. Then, thinking it over again, it seems to him that the president of the Commission for Children's Rights had also mentioned children living in precarious circumstances. Enough to reassure himself of the perfect legitimacy of his mission, since the entire town lives in precarious circumstances.

"This dilapidated building, you call that a school?" he says, snapping his ledger shut.

"You bombed us. Most of the time we live without water or electricity. Are you going to reproach us for our cracked walls and our gutted windows?"

"Even so, the children need care your institutions cannot provide, let alone in this region. We are showing boundless kindness and spending precious money to help them. You should be grateful and thank us; the government of Russia has a mission to protect minors, whatever their nationality."

Veronika is about to ask him how many children have died under the bombs his government dropped on hundreds of schools, maternity wards, hospitals, or public gardens, how many others are now orphans, but reason forces her to keep silent.

"My son does not need care, and least of all yours!" she replies in an icy voice. "The civilians your men shoot at point-blank range, on the other hand, are badly in need of mine."

"Well then, go back to your work," the officer shoots back.

"Where is my son?" Veronika insists again, ready to commit murder.

The man walks to the window and looks out at the street.

"On his way to a reception center. Our specialists will question him, and after assessing his condition, they will decide what is best for him."

"My son will not answer your questions. He cannot speak."

“And you were saying he needed no care? What kind of parent are you?”

Lilya sees the fire burning in her mother’s eyes. She takes her hand to remind her that she is by her side, then takes the initiative and addresses the officer.

“We don’t want to cause you any trouble, we just want to find my brother, know that he’s all right, and know where you’ve taken him. Please.”





The teenager’s self-restraint surprises the officer. He tells himself she will be easier to win over, might even help him persuade her mother to leave him alone.

“Your brother is fine, and we have not abducted him. We have merely put him out of harm’s way, to offer him the childhood he is entitled to, wholesome food that can no longer be found here and that he needs in order to grow, supervision that will allow him to keep up properly with his schooling. Even during a special operation like the one we are conducting right now, don’t you think?”

“A special operation?” Veronika flares. “It’s a war you’re waging against us!”

“That false designation is punishable by eight years in prison! We are not waging war on anyone, we are liberating Ukraine!” the officer objects, outraged.

“And where you come from, is mentioning rain when it’s raining also punishable by prison? A thunderstorm, you call that drizzle, mist?”

“When calm has returned and his safety is assured, we will send him back to you, I promise,” he says in a voice that clearly suggests his patience is at an end.

Veronika’s aplomb has stripped away his swagger, to the point that he feels pathetic; his superiority is slipping through his fingers like the sand on the beaches of Odesa he used to play with as a boy. His own mother must be ten years older than this woman, and if she had found herself facing him in similar circumstances, she would have given him a thrashing and booted him out of the room with kicks to the backside. This time, he will not let it happen. He is going to show who he is.





“One more word and I’ll have you arrested!” he says, adopting a grave tone. “Your daughter needs you, and if you could no longer look after her, I would be forced to place her in safety as well.”

“Where is this center?” Lilya asks.

“I don’t know yet. We have many of them; there are so many children in precarious situations in your country. Come back and see me in three days. I’ll know more then, and I’ll tell you where your son is, perhaps I may even be able to give you news of him, provided you do not make trouble before then.”

Terrified by the thought of being held in turn, and hoping the officer will keep his promise, Lilya grabs her mother by the arm and begs her to obey. They will get nothing more today except make their situation worse.
